metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay's payment validation service has 95% line coverage — yet a bug slipped through: the amount validation `amount > 0` was changed to `amount >= 0` (allowing ₹0 payments). Line coverage didn't catch it — the test checked that validation runs, not WHAT it validates. PITest introduces this exact mutation ('conditional boundary') and checks if any test fails. If no test fails: mutant survives → test gap found. After this topic, you can prove your tests actually catch bugs, not just execute code.

hook.what_you_will_build: PITest mutation analysis on payment validation code, identifying and killing surviving mutants.

concept_explanation.pitest_overview:
**PITest — how it works:**

```
PITest process:
1. Compile your code normally
2. Run your tests: all must pass (baseline)
3. Introduce mutation 1: change amount > 0 to amount >= 0
4. Re-run all tests: do any fail? YES → mutant KILLED (test catches the change)
5. Introduce mutation 2: return value → return true (always)
6. Re-run all tests: do any fail? NO → mutant SURVIVES (test gap!)
7. Generate HTML report: mutation score = killed/(killed+survived)
```
concept_explanation.maven_config:
**PITest Maven plugin:**

```xml
<!-- pom.xml -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.pitest</groupId>
  <artifactId>pitest-maven</artifactId>
  <version>1.15.0</version>
  <dependencies>
    <!-- For JUnit 5 support: -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.pitest</groupId>
      <artifactId>pitest-junit5-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>1.2.1</version>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>
  <configuration>
    <targetClasses>                           <!-- which classes to mutate -->
      <param>com.razorpay.payment.validation.*</param>
    </targetClasses>
    <targetTests>
      <param>com.razorpay.payment.validation.*Test</param>
    </targetTests>
    <mutators>                                <!-- which mutations to try -->
      <mutator>CONDITIONALS_BOUNDARY</mutator> <!-- > to >= etc -->
      <mutator>NEGATE_CONDITIONALS</mutator>   <!-- if(x) to if(!x) -->
      <mutator>VOID_METHOD_CALLS</mutator>     <!-- remove void calls -->
      <mutator>RETURN_VALS</mutator>           <!-- return true/false/null -->
    </mutators>
    <outputFormats><outputFormat>HTML</outputFormat></outputFormats>
    <mutationThreshold>80</mutationThreshold> <!-- fail build if score < 80% -->
  </configuration>
</plugin>

<!-- Run: mvn test-compile org.pitest:pitest-maven:mutationCoverage -->
```

```
PITest report output:
  Mutation Score: 85% (34/40 mutations killed)
  Survived mutants:
    PaymentValidator.java:23 - CONDITIONALS_BOUNDARY - SURVIVED
    PaymentValidator.java:45 - RETURN_VALS - SURVIVED
  → Fix: add boundary tests for line 23 and return value tests for line 45
```

visual_aid.description: PITest mutation cycle diagram. Center: Source code file. Left: PITest introduces mutations (4 boxes: conditional boundary, negate conditional, return value, remove void call). Each mutation spawns right-pointing arrows to 'Run tests' boxes. Green arrows: tests fail = mutant KILLED (good!). Red arrows: tests pass = mutant SURVIVED (test gap). Bottom bar: mutation score = killed / (killed + survived). HTML report shown with colored class-level breakdown.
visual_aid.alt_text: PITest mutation testing: introduce code mutations, rerun tests, count how many mutations are caught

worked_example.scenario: PITest on Razorpay payment amount validator.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. PaymentValidator with isValidAmount, isValidMerchantId
2. Run PITest: identify surviving mutants
3. Write tests to kill surviving mutants
4. Mutation score reaches 90%+

step_by_step_solution.details: CONDITIONALS_BOUNDARY: `amount > 0` → `amount >= 0`. Tests must check the BOUNDARY: `isValidAmount(BigDecimal.ZERO)` should return false. Without this test: boundary mutant survives. NEGATE_CONDITIONALS: `if (merchant != null)` → `if (merchant == null)`. Tests must test BOTH null and non-null. RETURN_VALS: replaces return statement with constant (true/false/null/0). Tests must verify the RETURN VALUE matters (not just that the method runs). VOID_METHOD_CALLS: removes a void method call. Tests must verify side effects of the void method.

things_to_notice: 100% line coverage ≠ high mutation score. A test can execute a line without verifying the line's correctness. PITest reveals which lines have MEANINGFUL test assertions.
Mutation score target: 80-90% is realistic. 100% is often impractical (some mutations in trivial getters/setters don't need tests). Configure targetClasses to focus on business logic.
PITest is slow (reruns all tests per mutation). Use targetClasses to scope only critical business logic. Avoid running PITest on every commit — weekly or per PR on changed classes.
PITest report: click on a class → see exactly which mutation survived at which line. Red = survived (test gap). Green = killed (tests good).

industry_examples.use_case: PITest runs on PaymentValidator, AmountCalculator, RefundEligibilityChecker (high-risk business logic). Mutation threshold: 85%. New PR: if mutation score drops below 85% on changed files, CI fails. Team: adds boundary tests before merge. Result: 3 production bugs caught by mutation testing in first quarter of adoption.

applications.interview_relevance: 'What's the difference between code coverage and mutation testing?' → Code coverage: what percentage of code was executed by tests. Mutation testing (PITest): what percentage of deliberate code CHANGES were caught by tests. 100% line coverage doesn't mean good tests — a test that calls a method without asserting anything gives 100% coverage but 0% mutation score. Mutation testing proves test quality, not just test quantity.
applications.real_world_workflow: PITest workflow: (1) add pitest-maven + pitest-junit5-plugin, (2) configure targetClasses (business logic only), (3) run `mvn test-compile org.pitest:pitest-maven:mutationCoverage`, (4) open target/pit-reports/index.html, (5) find surviving mutants, (6) add boundary/negative tests to kill them, (7) re-run PITest to verify, (8) set mutationThreshold in CI to gate PRs.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Running PITest on ALL classes (including generated code, DTOs, configs). PITest takes hours and produces thousands of irrelevant mutations.
common_gaps.remediation: targetClasses: scope to business logic only. Example: `com.razorpay.payment.validation.*` for validators, `com.razorpay.payment.service.*` for service layer. Exclude: DTOs, entities, configs, generated code. Rule: run PITest on code with complex conditional logic and business rules.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: PITest takes 30+ minutes or reports 10,000+ mutations — targetClasses is too broad

key_takeaways: Mutation testing: proves tests CATCH bugs, not just execute code
PITest: introduces mutations, reruns tests, scores how many mutations are caught
Killed mutant: at least one test fails when mutation applied (good!)
Surviving mutant: all tests pass despite the mutation (test gap)
RETURN_VALS: returns constant (catch with assertion on return value)
targetClasses: scope PITest to business logic only (not DTOs, configs)
PITest is slow: run on PRs, not every commit — or weekly CI

self_check_questions.question: A payment validator has 100% line coverage but a 55% mutation score. What does this mean and how do you fix it?
self_check_questions.answer: 100% line coverage: every line of the validator was executed at least once during tests. 55% mutation score: when PITest introduces 100 mutations (conditional boundaries, negations, return value changes), only 55 of them cause a test failure. The remaining 45 mutations pass all tests undetected. This means 45% of deliberate code changes would not be caught by the current test suite. Fix: look at PITest report → find surviving mutants. Common patterns: (1) CONDITIONALS_BOUNDARY on `amount > 0`: add test with `amount = BigDecimal.ZERO` (boundary value). (2) RETURN_VALS on a method: add assertion that checks the RETURN VALUE (not just that the method runs). (3) NEGATE_CONDITIONALS on `if (merchant != null)`: add test with null merchant. Goal: raise mutation score to 80-90% by adding boundary, negative, and return-value assertions.
